title: Tools for Cleaning Rectangular Data
text: dependency-free collection simple functions cleaning rectangular data. package allows detect, count replace values discard rows/columns using predicate function. addition, provides tools check conditions return informative error messages.

title: Tools for Cleaning Rectangular Data
text: can install released version arkhe CRAN : development version GitHub :
code: install.packages("arkhe") # install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("tesselle/arkhe")

title: Tools for Cleaning Rectangular Data
code: ## Load the package library(arkhe) ## Create a matrix X <- matrix(sample(1:10, 25, TRUE), nrow = 5, ncol = 5)  ## Add NA k <- sample(1:25, 3, FALSE) X[k] <- NA X #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    3    7   NA    2   10 #> [2,]    5    1   10   10    1 #> [3,]    1   NA   10    3    1 #> [4,]   NA    6   10    2    1 #> [5,]    7   10    3    5   10  ## Count missing values in rows count(X, f = is.na, margin = 1) #> [1] 1 0 1 1 0 ## Count non-missing values in columns count(X, f = is.na, margin = 2, negate = TRUE) #> [1] 4 4 4 5 5  ## Find row with NA detect(X, f = is.na, margin = 1) #> [1]  TRUE FALSE  TRUE  TRUE FALSE ## Find column without any NA detect(X, f = is.na, margin = 2, negate = TRUE, all = TRUE) #> [1] FALSE FALSE FALSE  TRUE  TRUE  ## Remove row with any NA discard(X, f = is.na, margin = 1, all = FALSE) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    5    1   10   10    1 #> [2,]    7   10    3    5   10 ## Remove column with any NA discard(X, f = is.na, margin = 2, all = FALSE) #>      [,1] [,2] #> [1,]    2   10 #> [2,]   10    1 #> [3,]    3    1 #> [4,]    2    1 #> [5,]    5   10  ## Replace NA with zeros replace_NA(X, value = 0) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    3    7    0    2   10 #> [2,]    5    1   10   10    1 #> [3,]    1    0   10    3    1 #> [4,]    0    6   10    2    1 #> [5,]    7   10    3    5   10

title: Tools for Cleaning Rectangular Data
text: Please note arkhe project released Contributor Code Conduct. contributing project, agree abide terms.

what: Convert row names to an explicit column — append
title: Convert row names to an explicit column — append
text: Convert row names explicit column

title: Convert row names to an explicit column — append
code: append_rownames(x, ...)  # S4 method for data.frame append_rownames(x, after = 0, remove = TRUE, var = "rownames")

title: Convert row names to an explicit column — append
text: x data.frame. ... Currently used. length-one numeric vector specifying subscript, row names appended. remove logical scalar: row names removed? var character string giving name column use row names.

title: Convert row names to an explicit column — append
text: data.frame.

title: Convert row names to an explicit column — append
text: N. Frerebeau

title: Convert row names to an explicit column — append
code: X <- data.frame(   x = 1:5,   y = 6:10,   z = LETTERS[1:5] )  ## Assign column to row names (Y <- assign_rownames(X, 3)) #>   x  y #> A 1  6 #> B 2  7 #> C 3  8 #> D 4  9 #> E 5 10  ## Append row names to data.frame (Z <- append_rownames(Y)) #>   rownames x  y #> 1        A 1  6 #> 2        B 2  7 #> 3        C 3  8 #> 4        D 4  9 #> 5        E 5 10

title: arkhe: Tools for Cleaning Rectangular Data — arkhe-package
text: dependency-free collection simple functions cleaning rectangular data. package allows detect, count replace values discard rows/columns using predicate function. addition, provides tools check conditions return informative error messages.

title: arkhe: Tools for Cleaning Rectangular Data — arkhe-package
text: arkhe uses following options() configure behavior: arkhe.verbose: logical scalar. R report extra information progress? Defaults TRUE.

title: Assign a specific row/column to the column/row names — assign
code: assign_colnames(x, ...)  assign_rownames(x, ...)  # S4 method for data.frame assign_rownames(x, column, remove = TRUE)  # S4 method for data.frame assign_colnames(x, row, remove = TRUE)

title: Assign a specific row/column to the column/row names — assign
text: x data.frame. ... Currently used. column length-one numeric vector specifying column number become row names. remove logical scalar: specified row/column removed making column/row names? row length-one numeric vector specifying row number become column names.

title: Assign a specific row/column to the column/row names — assign
text: data.frame.

title: Assign a specific row/column to the column/row names — assign
text: N. Frerebeau

title: Assign a specific row/column to the column/row names — assign
code: X <- data.frame(   x = 1:5,   y = 6:10,   z = LETTERS[1:5] )  ## Assign column to row names (Y <- assign_rownames(X, 3)) #>   x  y #> A 1  6 #> B 2  7 #> C 3  8 #> D 4  9 #> E 5 10  ## Append row names to data.frame (Z <- append_rownames(Y)) #>   rownames x  y #> 1        A 1  6 #> 2        B 2  7 #> 3        C 3  8 #> 4        D 4  9 #> 5        E 5 10

title: Bootstrap Estimation — bootstrap
text: Samples randomly elements object replacement.

title: Bootstrap Estimation — bootstrap
code: bootstrap(object, ...)  # S4 method for numeric bootstrap(object, do, n, ..., f = NULL)

title: Bootstrap Estimation — bootstrap
text: object numeric vector. ... Extra arguments passed . function takes object argument returns single numeric value. n non-negative integer giving number bootstrap replications. f function takes single numeric vector (result ) argument.

title: Bootstrap Estimation — bootstrap
text: f NULL, bootstrap() returns result f applied n values . f NULL, bootstrap() returns named numeric  vector following elements: original observed value applied object. mean bootstrap estimate mean . bias bootstrap estimate bias . error bootstrap estimate standard error .

title: Bootstrap Estimation — bootstrap
text: N. Frerebeau

title: Bootstrap Estimation — bootstrap
code: x <- rnorm(20)  ## Bootstrap bootstrap(x, do = mean, n = 100) #>     original         mean         bias        error  #> -0.178533538 -0.173236218  0.005297321  0.173307523   ## Estimate the 25th and 95th percentiles quant <- function(x) { quantile(x, probs = c(0.25, 0.75)) } bootstrap(x, n = 100, do = mean, f = quant) #>         25%         75%  #> -0.31142407 -0.07124923   ## Jackknife jackknife(x, do = mean) # Sample mean #>   original       mean       bias      error  #> -0.1785335 -0.1785335  0.0000000  0.1822873

title: Remove empty rows/columns — compact
text: Removes empty rows/columns array-like object using predicate function.

title: Remove empty rows/columns — compact
code: compact(x, ...)  compact_cols(x, ...)  compact_rows(x, ...)  # S4 method for ANY compact(x, margin = 1)  # S4 method for ANY compact_cols(x)  # S4 method for ANY compact_rows(x)

title: Remove empty rows/columns — compact
text: x object (matrix data.frame). ... Currently used. margin vector giving subscripts function applied (1 indicates rows, 2 indicates columns).

title: Remove empty rows/columns — compact
text: row/column empty contains NA, zeros (type numeric) zero length character strings (type character).

title: Remove empty rows/columns — compact
text: N. Frerebeau

title: Remove empty rows/columns — compact
code: ## Create a count data matrix X <- matrix(sample(1:10, 25, TRUE), nrow = 5, ncol = 5)  ## Add NA k <- sample(1:25, 3, FALSE) X[k] <- NA X #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]   NA    8    2    9    1 #> [2,]    4    6    5    4    2 #> [3,]   NA    3    9    2    3 #> [4,]   NA    2    9    9    2 #> [5,]    6    3    3    5    1  ## Count missing values in rows count(X, f = is.na, margin = 1) #> [1] 1 0 1 1 0 ## Count non-missing values in columns count(X, f = is.na, margin = 2, negate = TRUE) #> [1] 2 5 5 5 5  ## Find row with NA detect(X, f = is.na, margin = 1) #> [1]  TRUE FALSE  TRUE  TRUE FALSE ## Find column without any NA detect(X, f = is.na, margin = 2, negate = TRUE, all = TRUE) #> [1] FALSE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  ## Keep row without any NA keep(X, f = is.na, margin = 1, negate = TRUE, all = TRUE) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    4    6    5    4    2 #> [2,]    6    3    3    5    1 ## Keep row without any NA keep(X, f = is.na, margin = 2, negate = TRUE, all = TRUE) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] #> [1,]    8    2    9    1 #> [2,]    6    5    4    2 #> [3,]    3    9    2    3 #> [4,]    2    9    9    2 #> [5,]    3    3    5    1  ## Remove row with any NA discard(X, f = is.na, margin = 1, all = FALSE) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    4    6    5    4    2 #> [2,]    6    3    3    5    1 ## Remove column with any NA discard(X, f = is.na, margin = 2, all = FALSE) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] #> [1,]    8    2    9    1 #> [2,]    6    5    4    2 #> [3,]    3    9    2    3 #> [4,]    2    9    9    2 #> [5,]    3    3    5    1  ## Replace NA with zeros replace_NA(X, value = 0) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    0    8    2    9    1 #> [2,]    4    6    5    4    2 #> [3,]    0    3    9    2    3 #> [4,]    0    2    9    9    2 #> [5,]    6    3    3    5    1

title: Conditions — conditions
text: throw_error() stops execution current expression executes error action. throw_warning() generates warning message. catch_conditions() catch_message() handles unusual conditions.

title: Conditions — conditions
code: throw_error(.subclass, message, call = NULL, ...)  throw_warning(.subclass, message, call = NULL, ...)  catch_message(expr)  catch_conditions(expr)

title: Conditions — conditions
text: .subclass character string specifying class message returned. message character string specifying message returned. call call. ... Extra arguments. expr expression evaluated.

title: Conditions — conditions
text: N. Frerebeau

what: Confidence Interval for a Mean — confidence
title: Confidence Interval for a Mean — confidence
text: Computes confidence interval mean desired level significance.

title: Confidence Interval for a Mean — confidence
code: confidence(object, ...)  # S4 method for numeric confidence(object, level = 0.95, type = c("student", "normal"))

title: Confidence Interval for a Mean — confidence
text: object numeric vector. ... Currently used. level length-one numeric vector giving confidence level. Must single number \(0\) \(1\). type character string giving type confidence interval returned. must one "student" (default) "normal". unambiguous substring can given.

title: Confidence Interval for a Mean — confidence
text: length-two numeric vector giving lower upper confidence limits.

title: Confidence Interval for a Mean — confidence
text: N. Frerebeau

title: Confidence Interval for a Mean — confidence
code: x <- seq(from = -4, to = 4, by = 0.01) y <- dnorm(x)  confidence(y, type = "student") #>     lower     upper  #> 0.1151118 0.1345606  confidence(y, type = "normal") #>     lower     upper  #> 0.1151265 0.1345459

what: Count values using a predicate — count
title: Count values using a predicate — count
text: Counts values rows/columns using predicate function.

title: Count values using a predicate — count
code: count(x, f, ...)  # S4 method for matrix,`function` count(x, f, margin = 1, negate = FALSE)  # S4 method for data.frame,`function` count(x, f, margin = 1, negate = FALSE)

title: Count values using a predicate — count
text: x object (matrix data.frame). f predicate function. ... Currently used. margin vector giving subscripts function applied (1 indicates rows, 2 indicates columns). negate logical scalar: negation f used instead f?

title: Count values using a predicate — count
text: numeric vector.

title: Count values using a predicate — count
text: N. Frerebeau

title: Count values using a predicate — count
code: ## Create a count data matrix X <- matrix(sample(1:10, 25, TRUE), nrow = 5, ncol = 5)  ## Add NA k <- sample(1:25, 3, FALSE) X[k] <- NA X #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]   NA    2    4    2    1 #> [2,]   NA    5    1    8   10 #> [3,]    7    2    7   10   10 #> [4,]   10    9    4    5    6 #> [5,]    4    5   NA    5    5  ## Count missing values in rows count(X, f = is.na, margin = 1) #> [1] 1 1 0 0 1 ## Count non-missing values in columns count(X, f = is.na, margin = 2, negate = TRUE) #> [1] 3 5 4 5 5  ## Find row with NA detect(X, f = is.na, margin = 1) #> [1]  TRUE  TRUE FALSE FALSE  TRUE ## Find column without any NA detect(X, f = is.na, margin = 2, negate = TRUE, all = TRUE) #> [1] FALSE  TRUE FALSE  TRUE  TRUE  ## Keep row without any NA keep(X, f = is.na, margin = 1, negate = TRUE, all = TRUE) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    7    2    7   10   10 #> [2,]   10    9    4    5    6 ## Keep row without any NA keep(X, f = is.na, margin = 2, negate = TRUE, all = TRUE) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] #> [1,]    2    2    1 #> [2,]    5    8   10 #> [3,]    2   10   10 #> [4,]    9    5    6 #> [5,]    5    5    5  ## Remove row with any NA discard(X, f = is.na, margin = 1, all = FALSE) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    7    2    7   10   10 #> [2,]   10    9    4    5    6 ## Remove column with any NA discard(X, f = is.na, margin = 2, all = FALSE) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] #> [1,]    2    2    1 #> [2,]    5    8   10 #> [3,]    2   10   10 #> [4,]    9    5    6 #> [5,]    5    5    5  ## Replace NA with zeros replace_NA(X, value = 0) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    0    2    4    2    1 #> [2,]    0    5    1    8   10 #> [3,]    7    2    7   10   10 #> [4,]   10    9    4    5    6 #> [5,]    4    5    0    5    5

what: Find rows/columns using a predicate — detect
title: Find rows/columns using a predicate — detect
text: Finds rows/columns array-like object using predicate function.

title: Find rows/columns using a predicate — detect
code: detect(x, f, ...)  # S4 method for ANY,`function` detect(x, f, margin = 1, negate = FALSE, all = FALSE)

title: Find rows/columns using a predicate — detect
text: x object (matrix data.frame). f predicate function. ... Currently used. margin vector giving subscripts function applied (1 indicates rows, 2 indicates columns). negate logical scalar: negation f used instead f? logical scalar. TRUE, rows/columns whose values meet condition defined f considered. FALSE (default), rows/columns least one value validates condition defined f considered.

title: Find rows/columns using a predicate — detect
text: logical vector.

title: Find rows/columns using a predicate — detect
text: N. Frerebeau

title: Find rows/columns using a predicate — detect
code: ## Create a count data matrix X <- matrix(sample(1:10, 25, TRUE), nrow = 5, ncol = 5)  ## Add NA k <- sample(1:25, 3, FALSE) X[k] <- NA X #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    7    5    3    4    9 #> [2,]    4    8    4    8    5 #> [3,]    9   NA    4    7    5 #> [4,]    1    9    2    7   10 #> [5,]   NA   NA    9    3    4  ## Count missing values in rows count(X, f = is.na, margin = 1) #> [1] 0 0 1 0 2 ## Count non-missing values in columns count(X, f = is.na, margin = 2, negate = TRUE) #> [1] 4 3 5 5 5  ## Find row with NA detect(X, f = is.na, margin = 1) #> [1] FALSE FALSE  TRUE FALSE  TRUE ## Find column without any NA detect(X, f = is.na, margin = 2, negate = TRUE, all = TRUE) #> [1] FALSE FALSE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  ## Keep row without any NA keep(X, f = is.na, margin = 1, negate = TRUE, all = TRUE) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    7    5    3    4    9 #> [2,]    4    8    4    8    5 #> [3,]    1    9    2    7   10 ## Keep row without any NA keep(X, f = is.na, margin = 2, negate = TRUE, all = TRUE) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] #> [1,]    3    4    9 #> [2,]    4    8    5 #> [3,]    4    7    5 #> [4,]    2    7   10 #> [5,]    9    3    4  ## Remove row with any NA discard(X, f = is.na, margin = 1, all = FALSE) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    7    5    3    4    9 #> [2,]    4    8    4    8    5 #> [3,]    1    9    2    7   10 ## Remove column with any NA discard(X, f = is.na, margin = 2, all = FALSE) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] #> [1,]    3    4    9 #> [2,]    4    8    5 #> [3,]    4    7    5 #> [4,]    2    7   10 #> [5,]    9    3    4  ## Replace NA with zeros replace_NA(X, value = 0) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    7    5    3    4    9 #> [2,]    4    8    4    8    5 #> [3,]    9    0    4    7    5 #> [4,]    1    9    2    7   10 #> [5,]    0    0    9    3    4

text: N. Frerebeau

title: Remove rows/columns using a predicate — discard
code: ## Create a count data matrix X <- matrix(sample(1:10, 25, TRUE), nrow = 5, ncol = 5)  ## Add NA k <- sample(1:25, 3, FALSE) X[k] <- NA X #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    5   10    1    6    2 #> [2,]    2    8    9    5    4 #> [3,]    1    2    4    4   NA #> [4,]    4   NA   NA    8    3 #> [5,]    3    4    8    9    2  ## Count missing values in rows count(X, f = is.na, margin = 1) #> [1] 0 0 1 2 0 ## Count non-missing values in columns count(X, f = is.na, margin = 2, negate = TRUE) #> [1] 5 4 4 5 4  ## Find row with NA detect(X, f = is.na, margin = 1) #> [1] FALSE FALSE  TRUE  TRUE FALSE ## Find column without any NA detect(X, f = is.na, margin = 2, negate = TRUE, all = TRUE) #> [1]  TRUE FALSE FALSE  TRUE FALSE  ## Keep row without any NA keep(X, f = is.na, margin = 1, negate = TRUE, all = TRUE) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    5   10    1    6    2 #> [2,]    2    8    9    5    4 #> [3,]    3    4    8    9    2 ## Keep row without any NA keep(X, f = is.na, margin = 2, negate = TRUE, all = TRUE) #>      [,1] [,2] #> [1,]    5    6 #> [2,]    2    5 #> [3,]    1    4 #> [4,]    4    8 #> [5,]    3    9  ## Remove row with any NA discard(X, f = is.na, margin = 1, all = FALSE) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    5   10    1    6    2 #> [2,]    2    8    9    5    4 #> [3,]    3    4    8    9    2 ## Remove column with any NA discard(X, f = is.na, margin = 2, all = FALSE) #>      [,1] [,2] #> [1,]    5    6 #> [2,]    2    5 #> [3,]    1    4 #> [4,]    4    8 #> [5,]    3    9  ## Replace NA with zeros replace_NA(X, value = 0) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    5   10    1    6    2 #> [2,]    2    8    9    5    4 #> [3,]    1    2    4    4    0 #> [4,]    4    0    0    8    3 #> [5,]    3    4    8    9    2

title: Tools for working with infinite values — infinite
code: remove_Inf(x, ...)  replace_Inf(x, ...)  # S4 method for ANY remove_Inf(x, margin = 1, all = FALSE)  # S4 method for matrix replace_Inf(x, value = 0)

title: Tools for working with infinite values — infinite
text: x object (matrix data.frame). ... Currently used. margin vector giving subscripts function applied (1 indicates rows, 2 indicates columns). logical scalar. TRUE, rows/columns whose values meet condition defined f considered. FALSE (default), rows/columns least one value validates condition defined f considered. value possible replacement value.

title: Tools for working with infinite values — infinite
text: N. Frerebeau

title: Tools for working with infinite values — infinite
code: ## Create a count data matrix X <- matrix(sample(1:10, 25, TRUE), nrow = 5, ncol = 5)  ## Add NA k <- sample(1:25, 3, FALSE) X[k] <- NA X #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    3   NA    4    7   NA #> [2,]    4    9    9   NA    6 #> [3,]    4    8    9    9   10 #> [4,]    8    7    9    3    3 #> [5,]    8    7    8    6    6  ## Count missing values in rows count(X, f = is.na, margin = 1) #> [1] 2 1 0 0 0 ## Count non-missing values in columns count(X, f = is.na, margin = 2, negate = TRUE) #> [1] 5 4 5 4 4  ## Find row with NA detect(X, f = is.na, margin = 1) #> [1]  TRUE  TRUE FALSE FALSE FALSE ## Find column without any NA detect(X, f = is.na, margin = 2, negate = TRUE, all = TRUE) #> [1]  TRUE FALSE  TRUE FALSE FALSE  ## Keep row without any NA keep(X, f = is.na, margin = 1, negate = TRUE, all = TRUE) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    4    8    9    9   10 #> [2,]    8    7    9    3    3 #> [3,]    8    7    8    6    6 ## Keep row without any NA keep(X, f = is.na, margin = 2, negate = TRUE, all = TRUE) #>      [,1] [,2] #> [1,]    3    4 #> [2,]    4    9 #> [3,]    4    9 #> [4,]    8    9 #> [5,]    8    8  ## Remove row with any NA discard(X, f = is.na, margin = 1, all = FALSE) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    4    8    9    9   10 #> [2,]    8    7    9    3    3 #> [3,]    8    7    8    6    6 ## Remove column with any NA discard(X, f = is.na, margin = 2, all = FALSE) #>      [,1] [,2] #> [1,]    3    4 #> [2,]    4    9 #> [3,]    4    9 #> [4,]    8    9 #> [5,]    8    8  ## Replace NA with zeros replace_NA(X, value = 0) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    3    0    4    7    0 #> [2,]    4    9    9    0    6 #> [3,]    4    8    9    9   10 #> [4,]    8    7    9    3    3 #> [5,]    8    7    8    6    6

title: Jackknife Estimation — jackknife
text: Jackknife Estimation

title: Jackknife Estimation — jackknife
code: jackknife(object, ...)  # S4 method for numeric jackknife(object, do, ...)

title: Jackknife Estimation — jackknife
text: object numeric vector. ... Extra arguments passed . function takes object argument returns single numeric value.

title: Jackknife Estimation — jackknife
text: Returns named numeric  vector following elements: original observed value applied object. mean jackknife estimate mean . bias jackknife estimate bias . error jackknife estimate standard error .

title: Jackknife Estimation — jackknife
text: N. Frerebeau

title: Jackknife Estimation — jackknife
code: x <- rnorm(20)  ## Bootstrap bootstrap(x, do = mean, n = 100) #>    original        mean        bias       error  #> -0.43815209 -0.44979395 -0.01164186  0.21742925   ## Estimate the 25th and 95th percentiles quant <- function(x) { quantile(x, probs = c(0.25, 0.75)) } bootstrap(x, n = 100, do = mean, f = quant) #>        25%        75%  #> -0.5974873 -0.3034488   ## Jackknife jackknife(x, do = mean) # Sample mean #>   original       mean       bias      error  #> -0.4381521 -0.4381521  0.0000000  0.2207199

title: Keep rows/columns using a predicate — keep
text: N. Frerebeau

title: Keep rows/columns using a predicate — keep
code: ## Create a count data matrix X <- matrix(sample(1:10, 25, TRUE), nrow = 5, ncol = 5)  ## Add NA k <- sample(1:25, 3, FALSE) X[k] <- NA X #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    3    6    1    8    7 #> [2,]    2    4    3    2    6 #> [3,]   NA   10   NA    9    9 #> [4,]   10    3   10   10   10 #> [5,]    2   NA    4    7    7  ## Count missing values in rows count(X, f = is.na, margin = 1) #> [1] 0 0 2 0 1 ## Count non-missing values in columns count(X, f = is.na, margin = 2, negate = TRUE) #> [1] 4 4 4 5 5  ## Find row with NA detect(X, f = is.na, margin = 1) #> [1] FALSE FALSE  TRUE FALSE  TRUE ## Find column without any NA detect(X, f = is.na, margin = 2, negate = TRUE, all = TRUE) #> [1] FALSE FALSE FALSE  TRUE  TRUE  ## Keep row without any NA keep(X, f = is.na, margin = 1, negate = TRUE, all = TRUE) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    3    6    1    8    7 #> [2,]    2    4    3    2    6 #> [3,]   10    3   10   10   10 ## Keep row without any NA keep(X, f = is.na, margin = 2, negate = TRUE, all = TRUE) #>      [,1] [,2] #> [1,]    8    7 #> [2,]    2    6 #> [3,]    9    9 #> [4,]   10   10 #> [5,]    7    7  ## Remove row with any NA discard(X, f = is.na, margin = 1, all = FALSE) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    3    6    1    8    7 #> [2,]    2    4    3    2    6 #> [3,]   10    3   10   10   10 ## Remove column with any NA discard(X, f = is.na, margin = 2, all = FALSE) #>      [,1] [,2] #> [1,]    8    7 #> [2,]    2    6 #> [3,]    9    9 #> [4,]   10   10 #> [5,]    7    7  ## Replace NA with zeros replace_NA(X, value = 0) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    3    6    1    8    7 #> [2,]    2    4    3    2    6 #> [3,]    0   10    0    9    9 #> [4,]   10    3   10   10   10 #> [5,]    2    0    4    7    7

title: Tools for working with missing values — missing
code: ## Create a count data matrix X <- matrix(sample(1:10, 25, TRUE), nrow = 5, ncol = 5)  ## Add NA k <- sample(1:25, 3, FALSE) X[k] <- NA X #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    5    8   NA    9   10 #> [2,]    6    8   10    3    2 #> [3,]   10    7    9    5    8 #> [4,]    4   10    3   NA    7 #> [5,]    8    8    2    6   NA  ## Count missing values in rows count(X, f = is.na, margin = 1) #> [1] 1 0 0 1 1 ## Count non-missing values in columns count(X, f = is.na, margin = 2, negate = TRUE) #> [1] 5 5 4 4 4  ## Find row with NA detect(X, f = is.na, margin = 1) #> [1]  TRUE FALSE FALSE  TRUE  TRUE ## Find column without any NA detect(X, f = is.na, margin = 2, negate = TRUE, all = TRUE) #> [1]  TRUE  TRUE FALSE FALSE FALSE  ## Keep row without any NA keep(X, f = is.na, margin = 1, negate = TRUE, all = TRUE) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    6    8   10    3    2 #> [2,]   10    7    9    5    8 ## Keep row without any NA keep(X, f = is.na, margin = 2, negate = TRUE, all = TRUE) #>      [,1] [,2] #> [1,]    5    8 #> [2,]    6    8 #> [3,]   10    7 #> [4,]    4   10 #> [5,]    8    8  ## Remove row with any NA discard(X, f = is.na, margin = 1, all = FALSE) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    6    8   10    3    2 #> [2,]   10    7    9    5    8 ## Remove column with any NA discard(X, f = is.na, margin = 2, all = FALSE) #>      [,1] [,2] #> [1,]    5    8 #> [2,]    6    8 #> [3,]   10    7 #> [4,]    4   10 #> [5,]    8    8  ## Replace NA with zeros replace_NA(X, value = 0) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    5    8    0    9   10 #> [2,]    6    8   10    3    2 #> [3,]   10    7    9    5    8 #> [4,]    4   10    3    0    7 #> [5,]    8    8    2    6    0

title: Numeric Predicates — predicate-numeric
text: Check numeric objects: is_zero() checks object contains zeros. is_odd() is_even() check number odd even, respectively. is_positive() is_negative check object contains (strictly) positive negative numbers. is_whole() checks object contains whole numbers.

title: Numeric Predicates — predicate-numeric
code: is_zero(x, na.rm = FALSE)  is_odd(x, na.rm = FALSE)  is_even(x, na.rm = FALSE)  is_positive(x, strict = FALSE, na.rm = FALSE)  is_negative(x, strict = FALSE, na.rm = FALSE)  is_whole(x, na.rm = FALSE, tolerance = .Machine$double.eps^0.5)

title: Numeric Predicates — predicate-numeric
text: x numeric object tested. na.rm logical scalar: missing values (including NaN) omitted? strict logical scalar: strict inequality used? tolerance numeric scalar giving tolerance check within.

title: Numeric Predicates — predicate-numeric
text: logical vector.

title: Tools for working with zeros — zero
code: ## Create a count data matrix X <- matrix(sample(1:10, 25, TRUE), nrow = 5, ncol = 5)  ## Add NA k <- sample(1:25, 3, FALSE) X[k] <- NA X #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    7    6    8    7    1 #> [2,]    2    6    2    8    4 #> [3,]    9   NA   10   10    9 #> [4,]   NA    2    6   10    8 #> [5,]    7    2    1   NA    3  ## Count missing values in rows count(X, f = is.na, margin = 1) #> [1] 0 0 1 1 1 ## Count non-missing values in columns count(X, f = is.na, margin = 2, negate = TRUE) #> [1] 4 4 5 4 5  ## Find row with NA detect(X, f = is.na, margin = 1) #> [1] FALSE FALSE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE ## Find column without any NA detect(X, f = is.na, margin = 2, negate = TRUE, all = TRUE) #> [1] FALSE FALSE  TRUE FALSE  TRUE  ## Keep row without any NA keep(X, f = is.na, margin = 1, negate = TRUE, all = TRUE) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    7    6    8    7    1 #> [2,]    2    6    2    8    4 ## Keep row without any NA keep(X, f = is.na, margin = 2, negate = TRUE, all = TRUE) #>      [,1] [,2] #> [1,]    8    1 #> [2,]    2    4 #> [3,]   10    9 #> [4,]    6    8 #> [5,]    1    3  ## Remove row with any NA discard(X, f = is.na, margin = 1, all = FALSE) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    7    6    8    7    1 #> [2,]    2    6    2    8    4 ## Remove column with any NA discard(X, f = is.na, margin = 2, all = FALSE) #>      [,1] [,2] #> [1,]    8    1 #> [2,]    2    4 #> [3,]   10    9 #> [4,]    6    8 #> [5,]    1    3  ## Replace NA with zeros replace_NA(X, value = 0) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    7    6    8    7    1 #> [2,]    2    6    2    8    4 #> [3,]    9    0   10   10    9 #> [4,]    0    2    6   10    8 #> [5,]    7    2    1    0    3
